**HydroServer QC App History Design and Functional Specifications**

**Last Updated**: July 8, 2025

# **1\. Introduction**

The HydroServer data quality control (QC) web application is intended to provide a web-based graphical user interface (GUI) within which a user can select a HydroServer datastream for editing/correction and then perform manual edits on that datastream. Edits may include deleting erroneous values, inserting values, interpolating values, adjusting values, drift corrections, etc. Edited data are then saved to a new, quality controlled version of the data.  
   
The main GUI is a time series plot of the data where the user can interact with, select, and modify the data. A tabular view of the data selected for editing will also be provided. Each edit event (see definitions below) is automatically recorded in a history that provides a saved record of all of the specific edits that have been made to the source datastream. The history also serves as the definition of changes made to a source datastream that, when executed, produce an output destination datastream that is a new, quality controlled version of the source datastream. This document describes the design and functional specifications for data QC histories. A [separate functional specifications document](https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ikFirjbsrGQd80vDy4OCLFZaflCf7HVqa1IMlIwOBL4/edit?usp=sharing) describes the functionality and user interface for the HydroServer data QC App.

# **2\. Definitions**

**Datastream**: A time series of data values for a particular observed property, at a particular monitoring site, observed using a particular sensor, recorded using a particular unit of measure, and having a particular processing level.

**Destination Datastream**: The datastream to which QC edits are saved. Typically this is a new datastream with a different processing level than the source datastream and serves as the quality controlled version of the source datastream.

**Edit Event**: An edit event is an individual action taken by a user that performs a specific operation on a selected set of data values. Examples include delete, interpolate, adjust, etc. Edit events correspond to a QC function in the hydroserverpy package (HydroServer’s Python client package) and in the corresponding JavaScript functions within the HydroServer QC Web App.  
   
**Edit Session**: A set of Edit Events performed within a selected Datastream or within a subset of data selected from a datastream by an individual HydroServer user.  
   
**History**: A recorded set of edit sessions and their corresponding edit events that have been performed on a datastream to move it from one processing level to another. A history defines the transformations on a source datastream that produce the output destination datastream.  
   
**Processing Level**: An attribute of a datastream indicating the level of quality control or processing to which a datastream has been subjected.  
   
**Selection Event**: A selection event is an individual action taken by a user that selects a set of individual data values. Selections may or may not include contiguous values. Selections may be made by clicking on points, by using box or lasso selection tools, or by running filters that select data values based on rules created by a user.

**Source Datastream**: The datastream to which QC edits are being applied. Typically this is a raw datastream recorded by a sensor in the field.

**Version**: A copy of a datastream produced from a source datastream having a different processing level than the source datastream.

# **3\. Constraints**

HydroServer’s data model uses the concept of processing levels to specify the level of QC a datastream has been subject to and to help with versioning datastreams. While a common model is to use a processing level \= 0 for raw data and a processing level \= 1 for quality controlled data, HydroServer does not constrain the processing levels that organizations can define within the database. Thus, the HydroServer QC App cannot make assumptions about the meaning of processing levels in the QC workflow or that there is a numeric progression of processing levels. 

To address this, the QC App will adopt the concepts of a “source datastream” and a “destination datastream”. A source datastream serves as the starting point to which QC edits will be made, and a destination datastream serves as a new version of the datastream to which QC edits will be saved. HydroServer users may define their processing levels however they want, but a source datastream and a destination datastream derived from that source datastream must have different processing levels.

# **4\. Session-Based History Design**

A history is represented as a directed acyclic graph (DAG) with the raw source datastream at the beginning of the graph and the quality controlled destination datastream at the end (Figure 1). Edit sessions (the green and yellow boxes in Figure 1\) represent nodes in the graph. Like code changes within the Git version control system used for software development, edit sessions represent “commits” of changes made to a source datastream. Edges in the graph represent directed relationships and dependencies between the source datastream, edit sessions, and the destination datastream. The destination datastream represents the result of applying all of the edit sessions (“commits”) that have been made in the QC editing process.

Edges (the black arrows in Figure 1\) are determined by the range of time each edit session modifies (e.g., an edit session is defined by a set of edits to data values within a user-selected time range) and the sequence with which edit sessions are created. For example, in Figure 1, eight edit sessions have been defined to apply QC edits for a datastream having data within the year 2024\. Seven edit sessions have been committed and one is in progress. Edit sessions 1-5 are conducted on sequential, two-month time blocks of data with no overlap. However, edit session 6 represents changes made to the data after edit sessions 1 and 2 have already been committed and is, thus, dependent upon edit sessions 1 and 2\. Similarly, edit session 7 depends on all upstream edit sessions (1, 2, 3, 4, 6\) being committed before it can be committed. 

Edit session 8 represents an edit session that is currently in progress, but not committed. Because it has not been committed, the destination datastream representing the quality controlled version of the data can only exist up to the end of edit session 5, which represents the edit session with the most recent ending datetime. Once edit session 8 is completed and committed, the destination datastream can be extended to the end of edit session 8\. 

If a user wanted to make additional changes to the data for the month of September, a new edit session could be created that would only depend on observations from the source datastream for September plus any edits made during edit session 5\. All other data and edit sessions could be ignored making it easier for the QC App to manage then needing to load in every edit session ever committed between the source and destination datastreams.  
**![][image1]**  
**Figure 1**. HydroServer QC history design example.

# **5\. Creating and Modifying a QC History for a Datastream**

A QC history can be created for a source datastream in the HydroServer QC App using the following steps:

1. A user selects a datastream for QC editing. The datastream is added to the plot and displayed.  
2. The user toggles a button in the GUI to start editing the selected datastream.   
   1. The datastream is placed in edit mode.  
   2. Any existing editing history for that datastream is loaded and displayed. If no editing history exists, a new editing history is created for that datastream.   
   3. The user selects a contiguous subset (time slice) of the datastream to edit. The default subset will include the full datastream but must have a begin datetime and an end datetime.   
   4. These actions begin a new edit session to be recorded in the history. The edit session will record the set of edits made by the user to the selected datastream within the selected time slice.  
3. Each time a user makes a selection (selects a set of data values) and then clicks an edit tool (e.g., interpolate, delete, flag, etc.), the data in the plot is modified to reflect the edit, and a new edit event is added to the edit session within the history logging the following information:  
   1. The user that created the edit event (this may be stored at the edit session level given that a single user will perform an edit session).  
   2. The datetime that the edit event was performed.  
   3. The function that was executed (insert, delete, adjust, interpolate, flag, etc.).  
   4. The list of data values that was affected (the selection).

# **6\. Saving Histories and Edit Sessions**

A QC history is created within the HydroServer database when a user initiates QC for a source datastream (see section above). Edit sessions may occur over time as new data become available in the source datastream or as previous edit sessions are reviewed. The section above describes how an edit session is created. The QC App will support the following states for saving an edit session:

1. In Progress: The edit session is saved to the database, but is not materialized in the destination datastream. The purpose of this state is to enable a technician to perform QC editing on a set of data values and then have those edits reviewed and potentially approved by another user.   
2. Committed: The edit session is saved to the database and materialized in the destination datastream. 

# **7\. Business Rules**

The following business rules must be enforced by the data model for histories and by the QC App. It is important to note that HydroServer’s general functionality will not enforce these business rules. Thus, it is important that organizations using the QC App to record QC histories use not only the technical constraints established by the QC App and the data model for histories, but also develop QC rules and procedures to ensure these business rules are consistently applied by people.

## **7.1. Datastream Relationships**

1. Each quality controlled destination datastream will have a source datastream defined as its source (i.e., a raw datastream).  
2. A history must encode the identifier for the source datastream and the identifier for the destination datastream. The identifier for the destination datastream and its metadata will be created when the history is created. The metadata for the source datastream should be copied to the destination datastream, but the user should have to choose a different processing level for the destination datastream and may want to change other metadata elements by selecting from available metadata elements (e.g., sensor, etc.). The form should be similar to the “create datastream from template” functionality of the HydroServer data management app. Users should also be able to select an existing datastream as the destination datastream (in the case that it was created in the data management app or by some other means).  
3. The source datastream needs to be treated as a source of truth and must be immutable once QC has been initiated. New observations can be streamed in and appended to the source datastream, but existing observations within any time period that has been subject to a QC session cannot be altered or deleted outside of the QC history. This will require the backend to disable changes to datastreams that happen within the date range covered by a history \- should return a 400 error code with a message about not being able to push data to a datastream for which a QC history exists.  
4. Destination datastreams output from the QC process are updated only through QC edit sessions committed to the history. No direct modifications or other streaming is allowed. Similar rules would have to be implemented in the backend. It would be possible for multiple software applications to modify the history for a datastream, but all software creating modifications to a datastream for which a history exists must create edit sessions and commit them to the history.  
5. A destination datastream cannot be the target of multiple source datastreams/histories.  
6. A source datastream can serve as the source for any number of histories and destination datastreams.

## **7.2. Edit Sessions and History**

1. A history contains one or more chronologically ordered edit sessions.  
2. An edit session represents a unit of work by a user and has the following attributes:  
   1. A single user that created the session.  
   2. A date/time that the session was created.  
   3. A selected set of data values from a source datastream defined by a begin datetime and an end datetime.   
      1. Where there are no upstream edit sessions, this selection is made from the original source datastream.  
      2. Where there are upstream edit sessions, this selection is made from the datastream that results after upstream edit sessions have been committed.  
   4. A set of ordered edit events initiated by the user during the session on the set of selected data.  
   5. A status for the session (i.e., in progress or committed) and a date/time at which that status was assigned.  
   6. An optional comment by the user as to the purpose of the session (e.g., “Data QC edits for the year 2007” or “QC edits for January 2025”).  
   7. A dependency hash, generated at edit session creation representing any upstream committed sessions that affect the same time range.  
3. The end date of selection for an edit session cannot be in the future. This prevents the selected data from changing as new data are appended to a raw datastream.  
4. Edit sessions must be executed in chronological order to transform the source datastream (raw data) to the destination datastream (quality controlled data).  
5. Each edit event within an edit session has the following attributes:  
   1. Is created by and attributed to the user who created the session  
   2. Event type (e.g., interpolate, adjust, drift correct, flag)  
   3. Operates on a set of user-selected data values defined as an attribute of the event.  
   4. Is timestamped.  
   5. An optional comment by the user as to the purpose of the edit event (e.g., “Interpolated these values after consulting the field notes”).  
6. Edit events defined within an edit session must be executed in order.

## **7.3 Data Value and “Undo” Constraints**

Modifying the source datastream independent of a recorded history that has been created for that datastream will invalidate the input data and potentially all downstream selection and edit events. Similarly, modifying the steps of an intermediate edit session within a history (e.g., deleting an edit event from an intermediate edit session) would impact and potentially invalidate all downstream edits that follow. Thus, the following constraints are required to preserve the integrity of a QC editing history: 

1. Data values in a source datastream for which a history has been created must be immutable, and any edits must be made within an edit session recorded within the history for that source datastream. In other words \- observations that have participated in an edit session cannot be editable outside of the recorded history. Performing edits to observations that have participated in an edit session (including inserting data values within a time period for which an edit session has been conducted) will invalidate all downstream QC operations. The following are exceptions:   
   1. New data can be streamed in (appended to the end of the source datastream)  
   2. Historical data can be added (appended to the beginning of the source datastream)   
2. Rolling back (undoing) changes made during the QC process will be possible using two methods:  
   1. Undoing changes sequentially from the end of a history until the user reaches the operation that must be undone. Users would then have to perform the subsequent set of operations again. Users will also be able to roll changes back to any edit session within the recorded history and then start editing from there. This will effectively delete any edit sessions downstream of the edit session the user chooses to roll back to.   
   2. Create a new edit session at the end of an editing history that makes the desired edits so that the undo/delete does not have to be done. This preserves the full history and is the encouraged solution (rather than rolling back a bunch of edit sessions and having to redo them).

## **7.4. Dependency Hash and Conflict Management**

The dependency hash is a hash of all upstream edit session identifiers affecting the same time range as a new edit session. Dependency hashes are required to help with the following situations:

1. Incremental compilation: The full history graph does not need to be queried when looking at a specific time window of data. Only upstream edit sessions that impact the selected time window need to be queried and applied   
2. Conflict detection: If a user begins an edit session and, before they commit, another edit session affecting the same time range of data is committed by a different user, the dependency hash will no longer match. This allows the system to reject or warn about potential conflicts, similar to how Git handles diverging changes.

# **8\. Data Model for Histories and Database Implementation**

Histories will be stored in HydroServer’s database as time-based DAGs. This will preserve the definition of each edit session, each edit event within each edit session, and the dependency relationships between edit sessions. Storing histories in the HydroServer database with simple linear dependencies will help alleviate potential issues with the size of the histories becoming bloated over time (e.g., as they would if histories were stored in files) and will simplify querying of histories.

Figure 2 shows the data model structure for datastream histories. In this model, a record in the QCHistory entity represents a linkage between a source datastream and a destination datastream. A datastream cannot be the target of multiple histories. A datastream could serve as the source for any number of histories.

**![][image2]**  
**Figure 2**. Data model for HydroServer QC histories.  
   
A QCSession will have several edit events (labeled “operations” in Figure 2). Edit events could either be stored in a JSON field on the QCSession table (as shown), or in a separate QCOperation table (not shown). We will consider the complexity of the operations and whether JSON or a table is best suited to store them. Edit events within an edit session will be linearly ordered.  
   
The QCSessionDependency entity represents the edges in the history DAG. This table should only store committed session dependencies. Uncommitted sessions should not affect the dependency hash of other sessions (probably).

The QCSessionStatus entity records status changes for each session. Initially, four statuses will be supported: “In Progress”, “Commit Pending”, “Committed”, and “Commit Failed”. When a user creates a session, it will be considered “In Progress” until the user successfully commits it. Committing a session will change the status to “Commit Pending” which will signal HydroServer to attempt to perform the commit. Upon completion, the status will either be updated to “Committed” or “Commit Failed” with an error message, e.g. if the dependency hash changed before the commit finished.

# **9\. Committing Sessions to Destination Datastreams**

A distinction needs to be made between when a user submits a session to be committed and when the session is successfully committed. The status of a session is changed from "In Progress" to "Commit Pending" when a user finishes their work and chooses an option on the web user interface to commit that edit session. At this point HydroServer will queue a job to execute edit events from the committed session and save the results to the destination datastream. Because datastreams may eventually become very long consisting of potentially hundreds of thousands of data values with an extensive history of QC edits, jobs that perform commits may be computationally intensive and taxing to perform on the web server. Instead, these jobs may be performed by an asynchronous task management service to reduce computational load on the web server. Where this is the case, there may be a delay of seconds to minutes (depending on the size of the datastream and QC history) between when an edit session transitions from "In Progress" to either "Committed" or "Commit Failed".

A commit may fail if an error occurs during execution of session operations, or if the session dependency hash doesn't match the destination datastream dependency hash at the beginning or end of the commit transaction. In either case, a message containing error details will be communicated back to the user through the QC app. If the error was related to the dependency hash check failing, the QC app will fetch the updated datastream observations, regenerate the session dependency hash, and ask the user to review their session with the updated data before attempting to commit the session again. The QC app will periodically check the dependency hash and notify the user if another session is committed to the destination datastream that overlaps with the time period their session covers. At a minimum, the QC app will perform this check when the user closes the app and reopens it to continue working on a session. HydroServer will not attempt any automatic conflict resolution; users must review conflicts before re-committing a session.

# **10\. Data Management API Endpoints**

The HydroServer QC app will retrieve and manage histories via the HydroServer data management API. Thus, other software tools that require access to datastream QC histories can also access them through the data management API.

## **10.1. QC History**

The QC histories endpoints will allow users to view, create, and edit quality control history records between datastreams. Histories will be publicly accessible at these endpoints if both the source and destination datastreams are public.

***10.1.1. Histories***

**Endpoint**: /quality-control/histories  
**Methods**: GET, POST  
**Description**:

* GET: Retrieve all public histories and histories the user has permission to view.  
* POST: Create a new history.

***10.1.2. History***

**Endpoint**: /quality-control/histories/{history\_id}  
**Methods**: GET, PATCH, DELETE  
**Description**:

* GET: Retrieve a history with the given ID.  
* PATCH: Update a history. Editable fields are "description" and "paused".  
* DELETE: Delete a history and all associated sessions.

## **10.2. QC Session**

The QC sessions endpoints will allow users to view, create, and edit specific quality control sessions of a history. Sessions will be publicly accessible at these endpoints if the parent history is public.

***10.2.1. Sessions***

**Endpoint**: /quality-control/histories/{history\_id}/sessions  
**Methods**: GET, POST  
**Description**:

* GET: Retrieve sessions associated with a given history. Sessions are filterable by start and end time.  
* POST: Create a new QC session for a history.

***10.2.2. Session***

**Endpoint**: /quality-control/histories/{history\_id}/sessions/{session\_id}  
**Methods**: GET, PATCH, DELETE  
**Description**:

* GET: Retrieve details for a session.  
* PATCH: Update a session. Editable fields are "description" and "operations". Committed sessions cannot be edited.  
* DELETE: Delete a session. Committed sessions cannot be deleted.

***10.2.3. CommitSession***

**Endpoint**: /quality-control/histories/{history\_id}/sessions/{session\_id}/commit  
**Methods**: PUT  
**Description**:

* PUT: Queues a session to be committed. Once the commit is complete, the session status will be updated accordingly. Sessions are not considered committed until the destination datastream is successfully updated.

## **10.3. Observation**

Additional observation endpoints will provide more granular functionality related to quality control outside the context of histories and sessions if needed. All observation functionality will be provided in the context of one datastream at a time. 

***10.3.1. Observations***

**Endpoint:** /datastreams/{datastream\_id}/observations  
**Methods:** GET, POST  
**Description:**

* GET: Retrieve observations of a datastream. Observations can be paginated, ordered, and filtered. Users can choose to retrieve observations in either row-oriented, column-oriented, or verbose record formats.  
* POST: Create a single observation for a datastream.

***10.3.2. BulkCreateObservations***

**Endpoint:** /datastreams/{datastream\_id}/observations/bulk-create  
**Methods:** POST  
**Description:** 

* POST: Creates one or more observations for a datastream, provided in a compact row-oriented format. Several modes are supported.  
  * ***append***: Only allows observations after the last recorded observation in the datastream. (default)  
  * ***insert:*** Inserts new observations anywhere in the datastream, as long as the phenomenon times are unique.  
  * ***backfill:*** Only allows observations before the first recorded observation in the datastream.  
  * ***replace:*** Inserts new observations anywhere in the datastream. All existing observations within the phenomenon time range of the new observations will first be deleted.

***10.3.3. BulkEditObservations***

**Endpoint:** /datastreams/{datastream\_id}/observations/bulk-edit  
**Methods:** POST  
**Description:**

* POST: Adds or removes result qualifiers and/or qualifying comments to datastream observations in a given phenomenon time range.

***10.3.3. BulkDeleteObservations***

**Endpoint:** /datastreams/{datastream\_id}/observations/bulk-delete  
**Methods:** POST  
**Description:**

* POST: Deletes all observations for a datastream in a given phenomenon time range.

***10.3.4. Observation***

**Endpoint:** /datastreams/{datastream\_id}/observations/{observation\_id}  
**Methods:** GET, PATCH, DELETE  
**Description:**

* GET: Retrieve a specific observation of a datastream.  
* PATCH: Edit a specific observation of a datastream.  
* DELETE: Delete a specific observation of a datastream.

—--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------  
Information below this point is retained for discussion purposes but is not part of the functional specification  
—--------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

**Notes on Scripting Features**

We’ve discussed the possibility of saving changes made to a datastream as a JSON or YAML file that can be converted to a Python script instead of just saving the raw Python script. If we do this, we need to figure out how to let the user make and save custom Python calls in this JSON/YAML. If we use YAML, we can save a Python script as a string associated with a specific step. Here’s a rough AI generated example of what that YAML could look like:

`header:`  
  `datastream_id: "6ad16073-449e-496a-8e1a-55263072c342"`  
  `created_on: "2024-04-30 16:00:00"`  
  `created_by: "kenneth.lippold@usu.edu"`  
  `purpose: "Performing QA/QC on raw datastream."`  
  `timestamp_range:`  
    `start: "2024-04-30 08:00:00"`  
    `end: "2024-04-30 12:00:00"`

`steps:`  
  `- function: "insert_value"`  
    `timestamp: "2024-04-30 08:00:00"`  
    `parameters:`  
      `value: 25.0`

  `- function: "linear_drift_correction"`  
    `timestamp_range:`  
      `start: "2024-04-30 08:00:00"`  
      `end: "2024-04-30 10:00:00"`  
    `parameters:`  
      `drift_rate: 0.03`

  `- function: "fill_gap"`  
    `timestamp: "2024-04-30 12:00:00"`  
    `parameters:`  
      `method: "interpolation"`

  `- function: "apply_qualifier"`  
    `timestamp_range:`  
      `start: "2024-04-30 08:00:00"`  
      `end: "2024-04-30 10:00:00"`  
    `parameters:`  
  	`qualifier: "POWER_FAILURE"`

  `- function: "custom"`  
    `script: |`  
      `# Custom Python script example`  
      `df['value'] = df['value'].apply(lambda x: 1 if x < 0 else x**2)`

This format gives us some flexibility in allowing users to write their own blocks of Python code to run on the dataset while still being able to save and load those custom scripts back into the QA app.

The output script generated from the above YAML would look something like this:

`import pandas as pd`  
`import hydroserverpy`

`def main():`  
    `# Load the observations dataframe`  
    `datastream = hydroserverpy.datastream.get("6ad16073-449e-496a-8e1a-55263072c342")`  
    `df = datastream.get_observations_df(`  
      `start_time="2024-04-30 08:00:00"`  
      `end_time="2024-04-30 12:00:00"`  
    `)`

    `# Step 1: Insert value`  
    `df['value'] = hydroserverpy.insert_value(df['value'], 25.0, "2024-04-30 08:00:00")`

    `# Step 2: Linear drift correction`  
    `range_start = pd.to_datetime("2024-04-30 08:00:00")`  
    `range_end = pd.to_datetime("2024-04-30 10:00:00")`  
    `df['value'] = hydroserverpy.linear_drift_correction(df['value'], 0.03, range_start, range_end)`

    `# Step 3: Fill gap`  
    `df['value'] = hydroserverpy.fill_gap(df['value'], "interpolation", "2024-04-30 12:00:00")`

    `# Step 4: Apply qualifier`  
    `range_start = pd.to_datetime("2024-04-30 08:30:00")`  
    `range_end = pd.to_datetime("2024-04-30 09:30:00")`  
    `df['value'] = hydroserverpy.apply_qualifier(df['value'], "POWER_FAILURE", range_start, range_end)`

    `# Step 5: Custom script`  
    `# Custom Python script example`  
    `df['value'] = df['value'].apply(lambda x: 1 if x < 0 else x**2)`

    `# Print the resulting dataframe`  
    `print(df)`

`if __name__ == "__main__":`  
    `main()`

[Jeff Horsburgh](mailto:jeff.horsburgh@usu.edu)we met internally here and decided the following: 

**Rationale**: We are trying to build/maintain trust and be an authoritative source of water measurement data. Therefore, we need to let the public know the history of data value changes within the “reviewed” processing level. So the value-level history tracking should be accessible through APIs, not just the QC tool. It appears that we would need a new table/API endpoint that logs such changes. The QC tool and the history it provides, with the ability to undo changes, will be limited to internal staff, including commissioners. 

The daily-level data is used in apportionment and distribution, etc, so we feel it is sufficient to provide this data-level history tracking to the public at a daily level (PL2-a) (i.e., summary of changes done to the sub-daily data in PL1 for that day). The sub-daily tracking will be available for internal staff through the QC tool. 

For data storage and retrieval cost reasons, we may want to limit this tracking to select stations that are within distribution systems or high priority stations, not all of them. So the Data Manager will check a box for a data stream that triggers its daily level history tracking for public purposes. 

Tracking metadata changes, such as site name changes through time, appears to be outside the scope of this time series-based QC tool, but we may want to consider it as part of the “new table” that logs changes to data values.

NOAA seems to support metadata history tracking. See here.  
[https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/cdo-web/datasets/GHCND/stations/GHCND:USW00024127/detail](https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/cdo-web/datasets/GHCND/stations/GHCND:USW00024127/detail)  
![][image3]

This is probably what we want the QC tool to display to users when they review the history of changes. When the user clicks on the previous value, they can see the text (somewhere) that explains why this value changed etc.

![][image4]

[image2]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image3]: <data:image/png;base64,>

[image4]: <data:image/png;base64,>
